q: 10 divisions on the main scale of a Vernier calliper coincide with 11 divisions on the Vernier scale. If each division on the main scale is of 5 units, the least count of the instrument is :
explanation: The least count of a Vernier caliper is the smallest measurement that can be obtained with it and is determined by the difference in the measurement of one main scale division and one Vernier scale division. In this case, 10 divisions on the main scale coincide with 11 divisions on the Vernier scale. This means that 11 divisions on the Vernier scale are equal in length to 10 divisions on the main scale. Since each division on the main scale is 5 units, this is equal to: divisions on main scale units per division units 10 divisions on main scale \times 5 units per division = 50 units The length of 10 divisions on the main scale (or 50 units) is \therefore equal to the length of 11 divisions on the Vernier scale. This means that: division on the Vernier scale units 1 division on the Vernier scale = 50 units 11 Therefore, the least count of the Vernier caliper, which is the difference between one main scale division and one Vernier scale division, can be calculated as follows: Least count value of one main scale division value of one Vernier scale division Least count = value of one main scale division-value of one Vernier scale division Least count units units Least count = 5 units-50 units 11 Least count units units Least count = 55 units 11-50 units 11 Least count units Least count = 5 units 11 Thus, the least count of the Vernier caliper is units 5 units 11 Therefore, the correct answer is: Option A: $\frac{5}{11}$

q: A, B, C and D are four different physical quantities having different dimensions. None of them is dimensionless. But we know that the equation AD = C ln (BD) holds true. Then which of the combination is not a meaningful quantity ?
options: C B D - A D $2\text{ C}$
explanation: Given, AD = c ln (BD) As log is dimensionless. So, [BD] = 1 $\Rightarrow [B] = 1 [ D ] And [AD] = [C] Now checking options one by one (a) [B ^{2} C ^{2} ] = [B ^{2} ] [A ^{2} D ^{2} ] = $[A ^{2}]$ [B ^{2} D ^{2} ] = [A ^{2}]$ $\therefore$ This is meaningful. (b) ( A-C D ) is not meaningful. As dimension of A \neq dimension of C Hence (A-C) is not possible. (c) [ A B ] = [AD] = [C] \therefore A B-C is meaningful. (d) [ C B D ] = [ C ] [ B D ] = [ C ] 1 = [C] [ A D $2\text{ C}$ ] = [ A D ] [ D ] [ C ] = [ C ] [ D ] [ C ] = [D] As dimension of C and D are not same so it is not meaning ful.

q: A body of mass m = $3.513\text{ kg}$ is moving along the x-axis with a speed of $5.00\text{ ms}^{-1}$. The magnitude of its momentum is recorded as
explanation: Momentum, p = m \times v = 3.513 \times 5.00 = $17.565\text{ kg}$ m/s ≃ $17.6\text{ kg}$ m/s Here number of significant digits in m is 4 and in v is 3, so, p must have minimum (which is 3) significant digit. Note : In this case, since we are calculating the magnitude of momentum, which is given by the absolute value of the product of mass and velocity, we should follow the rules for significant figures as if we were multiplying the two values together. The least precise value in the calculation has three significant figures (the velocity), so we should round the answer to three significant figures as well. Therefore, the correct answer with three significant figures is $17.6\text{ kg}$ m/s.

q: A physical quantity P is described by the relation P = a $\frac{1}{2}$ b ^{2} c ^{3} d ^{-4} If the relative errors in the measurement of a, b, c and d respectively, are 2%, 1%, 3% and 5%, then the relative error in P will be :
explanation: Given, P = a $\frac{1}{2}$ b ^{2} ^{} c ^{3} d ^{-4} Relative error = $\Delta P/P \times 100 = ( $\frac{1}{2}$ \times \Delta a/a + 2 $\Delta$ b/b + 3 \Delta c/c + 4 $\Delta$ d/d ) \times 100 = $\frac{1}{2}$ \times 2 + 2 \times 1 + 3 \times 3 + 4 \times 5 = 32%

q: A physical quantity 'y' is represented by the formula y = m^{2} r - 4 g x l - $\frac{3}{2}$ If the percentage errors found in y, m, r, l and g are 18, 1, 0.5, 4 and p respectively, then find the value of x and p.
explanation: $\Delta y/y = 2 \Delta$ m/m+4 $\Delta$ r/r+x \Delta g/g+ $\frac{3}{2}$ \Delta l/l 18 = 2 ( 1 ) + 4 ( 0.5 ) + x p+ $\frac{3}{2}$ ( 4 ) $\Rightarrow$ 8 = xp By checking from options. x = $\frac{16}{3}$ , p = $\pm\frac{3}{2}$

q: A physical quantity z depends on four observables a, b, c and d, as z = a 2 b $\frac{2}{3}$ c d 3 . The percentages of error in the measurement of a, b, c and d are 2%, 1.5%, 4% and 2.5% respectively. The percentage of error in z is :
explanation: z = a 2 b $\frac{2}{3}$ c d 3 $\Rightarrow$ d z/z \times 100 = ( 2 d a/a+ $\frac{2}{3}$ d b/b+ $\frac{1}{2}$ d c/c+3 d ( d )/d ) \times 100 % error in z = ( 2 \times 2+ $\frac{2}{3}$ \times 1.5+ $\frac{1}{2}$ \times 4+3 \times 2.5 ) % = ( 4 + 1 + 2 + 7.5 ) % = 14.5 %

explanation: Least count of screw gauge = 0.$\frac{5}{50}$ = 1 \times 10 ^{-5} m = 10 $\mu$ m Zero error in positive.

q: A student determined Young's Modulus of elasticity using the formula Y = M g L^{3} 4 b d 3 δ . The value of g is taken to be $9.8\text{ m}$ /s ^{2} , without any significant error, his observation are as following. Physical Quantity Least count of the Equipment used for measurement Observed value Mass (M) 1 g $2\text{ kg}$ Length of bar (L) $1\text{ mm}$ $1\text{ m}$ Breadth of bar (b) $0.1\text{ mm}$ $4\text{ cm}$ Thickness of bar (d) $0.01\text{ mm}$ $0.4\text{ cm}$ Depression ( δ ) $0.01\text{ mm}$ $5\text{ mm}$ Then the fractional error in the measurement of Y is :
explanation: y = M g L^{3} 4 b d 3 δ $\Delta$ y/y = $\Delta$ M/M+3 $\Delta$ L/L + \Delta b/b+3 $\Delta$ d/d + \Delta δ/δ $\Delta$ y/y = 10-$\frac{3}{2}$ +3 \times 10-3 1+10-$\frac{2}{4}$ +3 \times 10-$\frac{2}{4}$ +10-$\frac{2}{5}$ = 10-3 [ 0.5+3 + 2.5+7.5+2 ] = 0.0155 Option (b)

q: A student measuring the diameter of a pencil of circular cross-section with the help of a vernier scale records the following four readings $5.50\text{ mm}$ , $5.55\text{ mm}$ , $5.45\text{ mm}$ , $5.65\text{ mm}$ . The average of these four readings is $5.5375\text{ mm}$ and the standard deviation of the data is $0.07395\text{ mm}$ . The average diameter of the pencil should \therefore be recorded as :
explanation: Given, d _{av} = $5.5375\text{ mm}$ $\Delta$ d = $0.07395\text{ mm}$ Significant rule says that reading should has same significant figure as that of reading given. ∵ Measured data are up to two digits after decimal. \therefore 5.5375 rounded to o 5.54

q: Assertion A : If in five complete rotations of the circular scale, the distance travelled on main scale of the screw gauge is $5\text{ mm}$ and there are 50 total divisions on circular scale, then least count is $0.001\text{ cm}$ . Reason R : Least Count = P i t c h T o t a l d i v i s i o n s o n c i r c u l a r s c a l e In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: A is not correct but R is correct.
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A.
A is correct but R is not correct.
Both A and R are correct and R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
explanation: Least Count = P i t c h T o t a l d i v i s i o n s o n c i r c u l a r s c a l e In 5 revolution, distance travel, $5\text{ mm}$ In 1 revolution, it will travel $1\text{ mm}$ . So least count = $\frac{1}{50}$ = 0.02

q: Expression for time in terms of G(universal gravitional constant), h (Planck constant) and c (speed of light) is proportional to :
explanation: Let t ∝ G ^{x} h ^{y} c ^{z} \therefore [t] = [G] ^{x} [h] ^{y} [c] ^{z} . . . . . (1) We know, F = G M^{2} R 2 $\Rightarrow$ G = F R 2 M^{2} \therefore [G] = $[M L T^{-2}]$ $[L^{2}]$ $[M^{2}]$ [G] = $[M^{-1} L^{3} T^{-2}]$ Also, E = hf \therefore [h] = [ E ] [ F ] = $[M L^{2} T^{-2}]$ $[T^{-1}]$ = $[M L^{2} T^{-1}]$ [C] = [ M o L T^{-1} ] From equation (1) we get, [ M o L o T^{1} ] = $[M^{-1} L^{3} T^{-2}]$ x $[M L^{2} T^{-1}]$ y [ M o L T^{-1} ] z [ M o L o T^{1} ] = [ M-x + y L^{3} x+2 y z T^{-2} x-y - z ] By comparing the power of M, L, T-x + y = 0 $\Rightarrow$ x = y 3x + 2y + z = 0 $\Rightarrow$ 5x + z = 0 . . . . . (2) - 2x-y - z = 1 $\Rightarrow$ - 3x-z = 1 . . . . (3) By solving (2) and (3), we get, x = $\frac{1}{2}$ = y and z = - $\frac{5}{2}$ \therefore t ∝ G h C 5

q: For the four sets of three measured physical quantities as given below. Which of the following options is correct ? (i) A _{1} = 24.36, B _{1} = 0.0724, C _{1} = 256.2 (ii) A _{2} = 24.44, B _{2} = 16.082, C _{2} = 240.2 (iii) A _{3} = 25.2, B _{3} = 19.2812, C _{3} = 236.183 (iv) A _{4} = 25, B _{4} = 236.191, C _{4} = 19.5
Given a charge q , current I and permeability of vacuum $\mu$ o ∗ . Which of the following quantity has the dimension of momentum ?
explanation: Let's look at the units (dimensions) of the given quantities: Charge Q has units of ampere\times time ( A ⋅ T ) Current I has units of ampere ( A ) Permeability of vacuum $\mu$ 0 has units of mass\times length\times time-2 \times ampere-2 ( M L T- $2\text{ A}$ -2 ) Momentum has units of mass\times length\times time-1 ( M L T^{-1} ) We want to combine Q , $\mu$ 0 , and I so that the result has the same units as momentum ( M L T^{-1} ) . Suppose the answer is Q x $\mu$ 0 y I z . Let's write out the units for this: Q x $\mu$ 0 y I z = ( A T ) x ( M L T- $2\text{ A}$ -2 ) y ( A ) z Combine the powers of each unit: Mass (M): exponent = y Length (L): exponent = y Time (T): exponent = x-2 y Ampere (A): exponent = x-2 y+z We want these to match the units of momentum, M^{1} L $1\text{ T}$ -1 : So we set up: y = 1 (so mass and length exponents match momentum) x-2 y = - 1 (time exponent) x-2 y+z = 0 (ampere exponent cancels out) Solve these equations: First, y = 1 . Second, x-2 ( 1 ) = - 1 ⟹ x = 1 . Third, x-2 ( 1 ) + z = 0 ⟹ 1-2 + z = 0 ⟹ z = 1 . The required combination is x = 1 , y = 1 , z = 1 : So, the quantity with the dimension of momentum is Q $\mu$ 0 I .

q: If e is the electronic charge, c is the speed of light in free space and h is Planck's constant, the quantity $\frac{1}{4}$ $\pi$ $\epsilon$ 0 | e | 2 h c has dimensions of :
explanation: Given e = electronic charge c = speed of light in free space h = Planck's constant We know, E = h c $\lambda$ and F = $\frac{1}{4}$ $\pi$ $\epsilon$ 0 q 2 d 2 $\Rightarrow$ q $\frac{2}{4}$ $\pi$ $\epsilon$ 0 = F d 2 1 4 $\pi$ $\epsilon$ 0 e 2 h c = F d 2 E $\lambda$ = F d . d E $\lambda$ = d $\lambda$ = dimensionless = $[M^{0} L^{0} T^{0}]$

q: If E, L, M and G denote the quantities as energy, angular momentum, mass and constant of gravitation respectively, then the dimensions of P in the formula P = EL ^{2} M ^{-5} G ^{-2} are :
If force (F), length (L) and time (T) are taken as the fundamental quantities. Then what will be the dimension of density :
If momentum (P), area (A) and time (T) are taken to be the fundamental quantities then the dimensional formula for energy is
explanation: Let [E] = K[P] ^{x} $[A]$ ^{y} $[T]$ ^{z} $[M L ^{2} T ^{-2}]$ = $[M L T ^{-1}]$ ^{x} $[L ^{2}]$ ^{y} $[T]$ ^{z} $[M L ^{2} T ^{-2}]$ = [M ^{x} ][L ^{x+2y} ][T ^{-x+z} ] Comparing both side we get, x = 1 x + 2y = 2 $\Rightarrow$ 1 + 2y= 2 or y = $\frac{1}{2}$ z - x = -2 $\Rightarrow$ z-1 = -2 or z = -1 \therefore [E] = [ P A^{1} $2\text{ T}$ -1 ]

q: If $\mu$ 0 and $\epsilon$ 0 are the permeability and permittivity of free space, respectively, then the dimension of ( 1 $\mu$ 0 $\epsilon$ 0 ) is :
explanation: The expression 1 $\mu$ 0 $\epsilon$ 0 is related to the speed of light c , given by the equation: c = 1 $\mu$ 0 $\epsilon$ 0 Therefore, we have: 1 $\mu$ 0 $\epsilon$ 0 = c 2 The speed of light c has the dimensions of L T L T^{-1} , where L L is the dimension of length and T T is the dimension of time. Thus, when squared, the dimensions become: L T L T c 2 = ( L T^{-1} ) 2 = L $2\text{ T}$ -2 Hence, the dimensions of 1 $\mu$ 0 $\epsilon$ 0 are L T L $2\text{ T}$ -2 .

q: If speed (V), acceleration (A) and force (F) are considered as fundamental units, the dimension of Young,s modulus will be:
explanation: We know, Young's modulus (Y) = F A $\Delta$ l l \therefore [Y] = $[M L T^{-2}]$ $[L^{2}]$ = $[M L ^{-1} T ^{-2}]$ Let [Y] = [V] ^{x} $[A]$ ^{y} [F] ^{z} \therefore $[M L ^{-1} T ^{-2}]$ = $[L T ^{-1}]$ ^{x} $[L T ^{-2}]$ ^{y} $[M L T ^{-2}]$ ^{z} $\Rightarrow$ $[M L ^{-1} T ^{-2}]$ = [ M ^{z} L ^{x + y + z} T ^{-x -2y - 2z} For dimensional balance, the dimension on both sides should be same. So, z = 1 x + y + z = -1 $\Rightarrow$ x + y = -2 ........(1) and -x -2y - 2z = -2 $\Rightarrow$ x + 2y = 0 ...........(2) By solving those two equations we get, x = -4 and y = 2 \therefore [Y] = V ^{-4} A ^{2} F ^{1}

q: If speed V, area A and force F are chosen as fundamental units, then the dimension of Young’s modulus will be
If surface tension (S), Moment of inertia (I) and Planck's constant (h), were to be taken as the fundamental units, the dimensional formula for linear momentum would be :-
explanation: We know, surface tension (S) = F L = $[M L T^{-2}]$ $[L]$ \therefore [S] = $[M T^{-2}]$ Moment of inertia (I) = mr ^{2} \therefore $[I]$ = $[M L^{2}]$ Planck's constant (h) = E f = Et \therefore [h] = $[M L^{2} T^{-1}]$ Also linear momentum (p) = mv = $[M L T^{-1}]$ Now we have to express p in terms of s, I and h. \therefore Let, [P] = [S ^{a} I ^{b} h ^{c} ] $\Rightarrow$ $[M L T^{-1}]$ = $[M T^{-2}]$ ^{a} $[M L^{2}]$ ^{b} $[M L^{2} T^{-1}]$ ^{c} $\Rightarrow$ $[M L T^{-1}]$ = [ M ^{a + b + c} L ^{2b +2c} T ^{- 2a - c} ] By comparing the dimensions of both sides, we get a + b + c = 1 .........(1) 2b +2c = 1 ..............(2) - 2a - c = -1 ...................(3) By solving those three equations we get, a = $\frac{1}{2}$ b = $\frac{1}{2}$ c = 0 \therefore linear momentum [p] = [S ^{$\frac{1}{2}$} I ^{$\frac{1}{2}$} h ^{0} ]

q: If the length of the pendulum in pendulum clock increases by 0.1%, then the error in time per day is :
explanation: T = 2 $\pi$ l g $\Delta$ T T = $\frac{1}{2}$ \Delta l l $\Delta$ T = $\frac{1}{2}$ \times 0. $\frac{1}{100}$ \times 24 \times 3600 $\Delta$ T = 43.2

q: If the screw on a screw-gauge is given six rotations, it moves by $3\text{ mm}$ on the main scale. If there are 50 divisions on the circular scale the least count of the screw gauge is :
explanation: Pitch = 3 $6\text{ mm}$ = $0.5\text{ mm}$ Least count = 0.5 $50\text{ mm}$ = $\frac{1}{100}$ = $0.01\text{ mm}$ = $0.001\text{ cm}$

q: If velocity [V], time $[T]$ and force [F] are chosen as the base quantities, the dimensions of the mass will be :
